techniques.evidence_quote: hackers were able to use Drizly's servers to mine cryptocurrency until the company changed its login information for its cloud computing account
a Drizly employee posted company cloud computing account login information on the software development and hosting platform GitHub
hackers were able to use Drizly's servers to mine cryptocurrency until the company changed its login information for its cloud computing account

attack_chain.description: A Drizly employee accidentally committed AWS cloud computing account login credentials to a public GitHub repository, exposing them to any external observer.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a Drizly employee posted company cloud computing account login information on the software development and hosting platform GitHub

attack_chain.description: Attackers discovered the publicly exposed AWS credentials on GitHub and used them to authenticate into Drizly's cloud environment as a legitimate account.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: hackers were able to use Drizly's servers to mine cryptocurrency until the company changed its login information for its cloud computing account

attack_chain.description: Once inside Drizly's AWS environment using the stolen credentials, the attackers used Drizly's cloud servers to mine cryptocurrency (cryptojacking).
attack_chain.evidence_quote: hackers were able to use Drizly's servers to mine cryptocurrency until the company changed its login information for its cloud computing account

aws_services: GitHub (third-party platform used for code hosting)
initial_access_vector: AWS cloud account credentials were committed by a Drizly employee to a public GitHub repository, allowing attackers to obtain valid cloud credentials without any exploitation of a vulnerability.
persistence_mechanism: Attackers used the valid, stolen AWS cloud account credentials to maintain access until Drizly rotated the compromised credentials.
privilege_escalation: No privilege escalation described; attackers leveraged credentials that already provided sufficient access to cloud compute resources.
